sections.paragraph:
fut. יַחְגֹּר to gird. [“Cognate roots, Arab.حجر to restrain. Syr. ܚܓܪ to lame.”] Construed variously-(a) with an acc. of the member girded, 2Ki 4:29 9:1 also with בְּ before that with which one is girded. So figuratively, Pro 31:17, “she girdeth her loins with strength.”-(b) with an acc. of the garment or girdle, e.g. חָגַר אֶת־הַחֶרֶב “he girded (himself with) a sword.” 1Sa 17:39 25:13 Psa 45:4, חָגַר שַׂק to gird himself with sackcloth, Isa 15:3 Jer 49:3. Part. act. 2Ki 3:21, מִכֹּל חֹגֵר חֲגֹרָה “of all that were girded with a girdle,” i.e. who bare arms. Part. pass. חָגוּר אֵפֹוד 1Sa 2:18, girded with an ephod; followed by a gen. Joe 1:8, חֲגוּרַת שַׂק “girded with sackcloth;” sometimes used elliptically Joe 1:13, חִגְרוּ “gird yourselves (with sackcloth).” 2Sa 21:16, וְהוּא חָגור חֲדָשָׁה “he being girded with a new (sword).” Metaph. Psa 65:13, גִּיל גְּבָעֹות תַּחְגֹּרְנָה “the hills are girded with joy” (compare verse 14 Ps6 5:14). Psa 76:11 (c) with a double acc. of the person and of the girdle, Exo 29:9 Lev 8:13 and with בְּ of the girdle with which, Lev 8:7 16:4 .-(d) absol. to gird oneself, Eze 44:18 1Ki 20:11. Here belongs 2Sa 22:46, וְיַחְגְּרוּ מִמִּסְגְּרֹותָם “and they shall gird themselves (and go out) from their fortresses;” unless the Syriac usage be preferred for explaining this passage, “they creep forth from their fortresses,” compare Mic 7:17 Hos 11:11.

Derivatives, חָגֹור, מֲגֹורָה, מַחֲגֹרֶת.
